back_translation: Now regarding the matter of food offered to idols — we know that "all of us have knowledge." But knowledge causes one to become puffed up; whereas love builds up.
If anyone thinks that he himself already knows some thing, that one does not yet know in the way that he ought to know.
But if anyone loves God, that one receives (royal) recognition-knowledge from God.
Therefore regarding the matter of eating food offered to idols — we know that "there is no idol that is anything at all in this world," and "there is no other God at all, except the one God."
Because even though there are things called gods, whether dwelling in the heavens or on the earth (just as there are many deities and many lords) —

yet for us, there is one God only — that is, the Father — who is the (royal) source of all things, and we exist for Him; and there is one Lord only — that is, Jesus Christ — through whom all things exist, and we exist through Him.

But not everyone has this knowledge. Some, because of their familiarity with idols up to the present moment, eat the food in a manner suggesting it is offered to an idol. And the conscience of those weak ones is defiled.
Food does not bring us close to God. If we eat, we do not become better; and if we do not eat, we do not become worse.
But take care indeed: do not let this right of yours become a stumbling-occasion for those who are weak.
Because if anyone sees you who has knowledge sitting and eating in the temple of an idol, will not the conscience of the weak person be drawn-out so as to dare to eat food offered to idols?
For the weak one — that is, the brother for whom Christ (royally) died — perishes through your knowledge.
When you sin in this way against brothers, and when you wound their weak conscience, it is equivalent to your sinning against Christ.
Therefore, if food causes my brother to stumble, I will not eat meat ever again, so that I myself will not cause my brother to stumble.
